table_of_content.title: Introduction
text: The relevance of the research topic is due to the impressive development of world trade and, as a consequence, the application of trade and investment law. The UN International Law Commission points out that «trade law is developing as a means of responding to the opportunities created by comparative advantages in international relations». Such statement has a solid ground, since the degree of globalization based on comparative advantages is the highest in the XXI century, which once again confirms the ideas of such classics of economic thought as Adam Smith and David Ricardo (the theory of absolute and comparative advantages). In support of this thesis, a number of well-known works on the degree of globalization of the trade sphere in the modern world can be cited: J. Federico and A. Tena-Junguito, and also M. Fouquin and J. Hugot.

The degree of globalization of world trade has increased compared to past centuries, and despite the skepticism of some economists about the economic approaches, calculations taken as a basis and the overall presence of an anti-trend in the form of «deglobalization», the thesis about inevitable globalization has already acquired the rank of an axiom. Moreover, globalization is a multifactorial phenomenon affecting not only trade, but also cultural, political and social aspects of life, therefore, acquiring a synergistic effect in the twenty-first century.

According to the WTO data, world trade volume today is roughly 43 times higher than the level recorded in the early days of the GATT (4300% growth from 1950 to 2021). It may be considered as evidence of a significant increase in world trade along with several studies above. Of course, COVID-19 and the recent political events have significantly affected the established international trading system, exacerbating already overdue flaws, for instance, the conflict of jurisdictions in international agreements. However, military conflicts and epidemics, although they are considered in many countries as force majeure circumstances, do not last forever, therefore, in this work, these phenomena are placed «outside the brackets» of the equation.

Conflict of jurisdictions is an essential problem that should be treated with all responsibility. To be more precise, the unresolved conflict of jurisdictions between international trade institutions around which the thesis is built, although it should be resolved, shall not be considered as an anomaly, rather an ordinary result of the confrontation of political and economic interests, framed in the legal field. Therefore, the main leitmotif of this paper is a neutral and objective analysis of the existing circumstances regarding the development of procedural general principles of law of the civilized nations, the resolution of the conflict of jurisdictions and its correlation with international trade.

Thus, the relevance of the paper can be abstractly deduced as follows. Firstly, the development of world trade and the improvement of economic activities is the result of increased degree of globalization and this is indirectly intertwined in our daily life. Secondly, the lack of extensive and in-depth research on the topic of general principles of law in international economic disputes since the middle of the XX century creates misunderstandings and dismissive attitude towards general principles of law. Thirdly, the presence of conflicts of jurisdiction in international economic disputes, which are resolved differently in such sub-fields of international economic law as investment and trade sub-fields of law.

The purpose of the study is to identify the features of the application and correlation of the procedural general principles of law in the investment and trade sub-fields of international economic law for the potential subsequent implementation of legal remedies in practice.
To achieve this goal the following tasks are performed:
to analyze the essence and give a description of the procedural general principles of law applied in the international trade and investment sub-fields of law;
to study relevant case-law for resolving international investment and trade disputes;
describe the potential and some pitfalls of applying procedural general principles of law in the above-mentioned areas of law.

The degree of scientific development of the research topic. There are some informative and in-depth works on the topic of research. Of particular importance in the process of research were the works Dumberry P., Fitzmaurice G., Cheng B., Kotuby Jr C. T., Nguyen S. T.. Marcelo V-B. (known as Special Rapporteur of UN),  Xuan S. and others.
A great contribution in this field of research belongs to professor Kuijper P.J. and Vidigal G., since they made reasoned assumptions about the subject of the study.
The object of the study is international legal relations arising in connection with the settlement of international economic disputes both between states and between economic entities.

The subject of the study is the application of the general principles of law in international economic law, namely, comparative characteristics, comparison of the experience of applying this source of law in two sub-fields: international trade and international investment sub-fields of law.
The methodological basis of the study consists of general scientific and private scientific research methods. Among the special methods are comparative-legal, economic-legal, formal-legal and others.

The structure of the thesis is determined by the subject, objectives, as well as the purpose of the study. The work consists of three chapters. The introduction reveals the relevance, determines the degree of scientific development of the topic, object, subject, purpose, objectives and methods of research, the explanation of the structure of the course work.

The first chapter examines the general characteristics of the general principles of law, the purpose, tasks, and perception of this category of law by the international scientific community. Attention is also paid to the correlation of general principles of law with other sources of international law. After considering the concept as a whole, specific examples are to be analysed and described in the next chapter.

The second chapter describes through examples the experience of applying procedural general principles of law in international trade and investment disputes, their impact on further consideration of the case. The main goal of this chapter is to emphasize the ways and degree of applicability of general principles in both above mentioned fields of law which are considered as polar (private for investments law versus public for trade law). Choice of examples was dictated by, firstly, procedural essence and, secondly, a search for common grounds. Thus, all of them are procedural and observable in both investments and trade fields of international economic law.
In the third chapter, by comparing the data from the previous chapters, the prospects and risks of applying general principles of law in international trade disputes were formulated and summarized.
In conclusion, the results of the study are described, the final conclusions on the topic under consideration are formed.

table_of_content.title: Chapter 1 General characteristics of the general principles of law recognized by civilized nations in international law

sub_elements.title: 1.1 Definition, essence and traits of general principles of law
text: Under the principles in the legal coordinate system, it is customary to understand «basic ideas», «starting points», or «that which in itself is not a rule, but which underlies the rule and explains its meaning». One of the sources of international law referred to in article 38 (1) of the Statute of the International Court of Justice of the United Nations is the «general principles of law recognized by civilized peoples» (paragraph c). Unfortunately, like customary international law, the scope of the general principles of law recognized by civilized nations (hereinafter referred to as the general principles of law) is disputed. Nevertheless, the general principles of law are indicated as the main, and not an auxiliary source of law for the countries participating in the UN Charter, such as «judicial decisions and doctrines of the most outstanding qualified specialists in public law of various nations» (paragraph d of the UN Charter).

Probably, the thought of the drafters of the ICJ Statute regarding the basic but not auxiliary nature of the general principles was as follows: auxiliary sources will not be able to become a solid basis for law enforcement and by themselves, without international treaties and customs, while the general principles of law act both as the main and as auxiliary source depending on the needs, conditions of a specific legal situation and field of international law.

Despite the fundamental nature, some scholars call the general principles of law «an abandoned source and just wall flowers on the sidelines of international legal arguments». To agree with this would be a mistake, and this paper is, in fact, a refutation of such thesis.

There are two theoretical approaches to understanding the general principles of law, which in one way or another go back to Roman law and to jus gentium, in particular. The first is the assumption that the general principles of law are inherently fundamental to all systems and demonstrate the essential and reasonable unity of the law. The second one is based on the data of comparative jurisprudence and comes down to a «general consensus» on the general principles of law. Both approaches emphasize the great importance of the general principles of law and are in solidarity about the natural-legal background of such.

Some researchers consider the general principles as a «Trojan horse», which could significantly expand the scope of judicial discretion and even establish judicial law-making. This is not a groundless fear, since, for example, the notorious conflict and the subsequent crisis around the WTO Appellate Body was caused to a large extent by «judicial activism». It is difficult to accurately predict how the active introduction of general principles of law into the process of considering international economic disputes can affect, but it may have a beneficial effect, which will be discussed in the second chapter.

In order to define the general principles of law, it is necessary to draw a line in advance between national principles and general principles of law. Regarding the first one it shall be noted that they are intrinsic too, but may vary from state to state. In the case of the identification of the latter the doctrine is a crucial assistant, in which, for instance, following statement may be observed: «in order to be considered «general», the principle must have such an increased degree of reasonableness that all parties recognize its normative value in advance, whatever point of view they choose after the dispute arises», the so-called «implied consent». From a law enforcement point of view, such doctrinal statements only create problems with their excessive philosophical burden. Therefore, in our opinion, it is worth using parts to recreate the whole.
The following features of general principles of law are distinguished:
they are part of international law;
they are (more or less) autonomous;
they promote the creativity of judges;
they are fundamental to the legal system;
they can be 'ideas', so they have a philosophical aspect, but a better term would be 'normative concepts' because they show that general principles are law;
they are only one of three sources and one of the elements that unite the system of international law;
they are a reservoir [of norms preventing lacunae].

General principles of law are thus defined as «... an autonomous, consensually created, systematically fundamental part of international law, consisting of various normative concepts that judges invoke through a creative process in order to promote the coherence of international law». In opinion of the author of this paper, this definition seems to be quite appropriate and complete in comparison to the others, and will therefore be taken as a basis for this paper.

Nevertheless, the general principles of law recognised by civilised nations contain a number of pitfalls which are worth keeping in mind when identifying the boundaries of the definition. Firstly, the construct of «civilised nations» is anachronistic as this discriminatory division is currently not taken into account. Secondly, as regards the «recognition» of general principles of law, there is certainty only as regards those principles that emanate from domestic law. For these general principles, as the Special Rapporteur Mr. Vázquez-Bermúdez has pointed out, the requirement of «recognition by civilised nations» is met by two conditions: 1) the availability of the principle in the legal systems of the world and 2) the suitability of the principle for application in the international legal system, the so-called «transposition».

The Special Rapporteur concluded that the criterion for transferring the principle to the international legal sphere is that the principle in question must be «compatible with the fundamental principles of international law» and that «there are conditions for its proper application in the international legal system». This does not add clarity to the process, but on the other hand, guidelines in the form of compatibility with international law are already emerging. Probably, doctrinal disputes will become the main engine for resolving this problem.

sub_elements.title: 1.2 Comparison and distinction with other sources of international law
text: The general principles of law shall not be considered as something almost identical to an international custom or even to an international treaty, despite the similarity in some aspects. Scientists Olufemi Elias and Ching Lim note that there are several criteria by which general principles of law can be distinguished from other sources, especially from international custom. To do this, it is proposed to use hierarchical and logical criteria. Hierarchical is that the general principles of law are something fundamental. As for the logical criterion, the view that the phrase 'general principles of law' refers to (or includes) (i) equity, (ii) principles of municipal law, or (iii) legal norms which have not satisfied the formal requirement of treaties and custom (inchoate custom), are all arguments that general principles possess an altogether different logical form from treaties and custom.

Provisional summary record of the 3489th meeting (2019) provided an opinion of the International Law Commission member Mr. Tladi that «General principles of law as a source of law might be described as customary international law minus opinio juris, or customary international law based purely on practice». As reasonably states Xuan Shao, «for general principles formed within the international legal system, the risk that they become “custom lite” is overrated. The “implied consent” of states underlying these general principles can be understood as an “incomplete consensus” among states, whereby they share a commitment at a general level without agreeing on particular solutions in specific cases. Thus, when applying these general principles, states should be accorded a relatively large measure of discretion and be deemed in violation of the law only when they act outside of the broad bounds of reasonableness. When this is followed in practice, the danger of making general principles of the second category an easier route of creating obligations for States would not arise, while general principles can still meet the systemic needs of international law».

Also, authors claim that customs and treaties are consensual forms of sources, while general principles of law are non-consensual ones. Moreover, since general principles can «harden» into treaty or customary rules, their «gap-filling» function can be more significant and consequential than it may appear. Therefore, the two sources of international law are separated based on the scope of application and other criteria, although they have similarities.

Another problem that was raised by Special Rapporteur is the issue of identifying general principles, namely, where should the principles derive from: from national legal systems or from the international legal system? The answer to this question will determine the procedure for identifying the general principles of law to be applied.

Drawing on a wide range of case law, the practice of international courts and tribunals, Special Rapporteur notes that general principles of law can be found both in national legal systems and in international law. In support of this, a number of cases are cited, for example, for national legal systems: Right of Passage over Indian Territories (Portugal vs India), Certain Property case (Lichtenstein vs Germany), Lighthouses arbitration (France and Greece), and in the international system: Corfu Channel case, Military and Paramilitary Activities in and against Nicaragua (Nicaragua v. United States of America), etc.

For instance, within the framework of the Certain Property case Liechtenstein noted that a principle common to national legal systems must be “transposable” to international law, and explained, inter alia, that there is “no incompatibility between the unjust enrichment principle and public international law” and that it “is received in public international law.  With regard to Corfu Channel case another remarkable notion may be added: «This indirect evidence is admitted in all systems of law, and its use is recognized by international decisions». By «all systems of law» both national and international legal systems may be, therefore, considered.

Nevertheless, it would not be superfluous to mention that most of the principles of the above cases relate to material ones, so a detailed analysis of them will not be set out in the thesis. However, the second chapter will elaborate on procedural general principles of law. Thus, the examination of the above cases made it possible to cursory estimate the importance of the general principles of law for the international dispute resolution system and approve that the general principles of law can be found both in national legal systems and in international law.

sub_elements.title: 1.3 Classification, purpose and objectives of the general principles of law in law enforcement
text: In practice, the appeal to the principles of law means that there is a legal lacuna and their application is a logical continuation of the prohibition of non liquet. In other words, the task of the general principles of law, as one of the sources of international law, is to «cover» legal gaps. However, some researchers also note the second case of the application of general principles of law – «when other sources of law (customs and treaties) have reached such a level of complexity and number of rules that the judge needs to agree on some form to determine which rule to use first and what validity he should give to each». Nevertheless, the fact remains that this phenomenon is directly or indirectly woven into the pages of court cases, affecting the content of decisions, so it is not difficult to find traces of general principles of law, which will be demonstrated in the second chapter of this paper.

B. Cheng fixed the general principles of law through their use by international courts and tribunals and listed some of them. Most of them did not come as a surprise, for example: good faith (paragraph 3 of Article 1 of the Civil Code of the Russian Federation and article 1104 of the French Civil Code), pacta sunt servanda, as well as a ban on the malicious exercise of rights (embodied in Russian law – Article 10 of the Civil Code of the Russian Federation, known as «shikana» in Russian doctrine or «nuisance» in English law) and others. B. Cheng did not neglect part of principles of judicial proceedings, for example, jura novit curia, audiatur et altera pars, nemo judex in causa propria, res judicata and others.

There are many classifications of general principles of law. Thus, the well-known German jurist H. Mosler distinguishes three categories of general principles, namely «principles taken from generally recognized national law», «general principles arising from international relations» and «general principles applicable to all types of legal relations». In each category, he sees various examples that create subcategories and still tries to compile an almost exhaustive list of general principles.

Kotuby Jr. C.T. argues that the application of general principles of law is divided into three categories: interpretive (interpretation), definitive (to determine the depth and contours of broad or amorphous legal norms) and corrective. The latter consist in the most frequent use of general principles and consist in the elimination of alleged shortcomings in applicable laws. As noted by some researchers, there was uncertainty about the application of general principles of law in relation to international organizations, since the latter do not participate in the creation of these principles.

The classification of Friedmann Wolfgang seems to be the most understandable and realistic, which sounds as follows: 1) «principles of approach and interpretation of all types of legal relations», 2) «minimum standards of procedural justice» and 3) «basic principles of law, widely and firmly recognized enough in the leading world systems to be considered international legal principles». Having reduced it, it can be noted that there are procedural, material and interpretive principles, as rightly noted by M. Panezi . This division that will form the basis for further discourse in this paper.

Regarding the tasks that are performed by the general principles of law, it is worth mentioning the «functional approach» derived by M. Bassiouni. In the context of this approach, M. Bassiouni, based on the works of B. Cheng, identifies the following tasks (functions):
A source of interpretation for conventional and customary international law;
A means for developing new norms of conventional and customary international law;
A supplemental source to conventional and customary international law; and,
A modifier of conventional and customary international rules.

If there are no special questions about the first three functions, then the last one provokes questions about what exactly the researcher meant. M. Bassiouni notes that this function is the most controversial, probably due to the fact that it is built around the concept of jus cogens. The essence lies in the presence of an imperative norm, which, by virtue of the highest status in the hierarchy, is able to modify lower norms and provisions. It is noteworthy that some reports of the International Law Commission noted the absence of any hierarchical relationship between international treaties, customs and general principles of law, however, due to the development of the system of international law, the need that arose was satisfied by the above hierarchy of norms.

Talking about goals of general principles of law and returning to the phrase about the «abandoned argument and wall flowers», a quote of the noble comparativist-scholar shall be mentioned. Rudolf Schlesinger wrote in his article: «Even if there is a treaty, its interpretation may require the application of general principles of law recognized by civilized nations. This is particularly true of the numerous treaties which employ broad language such as «freedom of access to the courts» or «most constant protection and security for their persons and property». As an example, he cited the Peace Treaty of February 10, 1947 between Italy, Bulgaria, Rumania and Hungary, which included obligations «to secure to all persons under their jurisdiction . . . the enjoyment of human rights and of the fundamental freedoms, including freedom of expression, of press and publication, of religious worship, of political opinion and of public meeting».

In this context, the history of the development and application of general principles of law highlighted in the report of the Special Rapporteur of the International Law Commission is of interest. Of course, the Special Rapporteur cannot cover the entire spectrum of this source, but the analysis is fascinating. The time period taken as a basis is the XIX-XXI centuries, and the coverage of institutions – starting with the practice of the Permanent Court of International Justice (and a little before its foundation), including the practice of the International Court of Justice, international tribunals, the European Court of Human Rights, European Court of Justice, Inter-American Court of Human Rights, International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes and ending with the WTO case-law. In particular, the law enforcement of the res judicata principle has been repeatedly mentioned, which will be discussed later in this paper.

As a conclusion, the Special Rapporteur put forward a thesis consistent with the main agenda of this work: «since the adoption of the Statute of the Permanent Court of International Justice in 1920, States and international courts and tribunals have referred to this source of international law on several occasions and in different contexts, leaving no doubt as to its relevance for the international legal order».

In the subsequent second and third reports, the Special Rapporteur M. Vazquez-Bermudez reveals no less significant problems, namely the question of the functions of general principles of law, the relationship with other sources of law, the issues of identification and recognition of general principles of law. Since the contents of these reports somehow repeat the theses and practices indicated in this paper, the reports are not subject to further analysis.

Thus, concluding the chapter, the following can be said. General principles of law have a number of functions, the main of which is filling in gaps in such legal situations where there is no clear understanding of the application of international treaties and customs, at the same time, general principles of law have a system–forming character for the institute of sources of international law, as they create a «framework», have a fundamental character and are recognized by all or almost by all states of the world due to their reasonableness and the presence of common sense in certain principles.

table_of_content.title: Chapter 2 Application of general principles of law in international trade and investment law
text: Politics, law, and economics do not exist in a vacuum, and conflicts do not arise out of nowhere. As a result of a number of categorical refusals of the States parties to the Marrakesh Agreements in personnel matters, as of September 2020, only one of the seven WTO judges were in the Appellate Body, which made it impossible to consider new cases in the second instance. The danger of «delaying disputes» for an indefinite period by a party that does not want to comply with the WTO DSB decision is obvious. This trend is noticeable in cases DS484: «Indonesia — Measures Concerning the Importation of Chicken Meat and Chicken Products», and also DS533: United States — Countervailing Measures on Softwood Lumber from Canada; DS543: United States — Tariff Measures on Certain Goods from China.

The «consistency» and «predictability» of the WTO dispute resolution system are significantly wounded due to the prolonged crisis, and Covid-19 has only worsened the situation. In view of the protracted WTO crisis, some states will probably want to turn to regional trade agreements (hereinafter referred to as RTAs), using their jurisdiction and mechanisms similar to those of the WTO. Nevertheless, the conflict of WTO and RTA jurisdictions, which is being resolved in favor of the WTO in some cases, could be an obstacle to dispute resolution, since the WTO faces challenges in resolving them at the moment. A potential solution to the conflict of jurisdictions for international trade law (in particular, WTO law) may be the general principles of law, so the latter need a detailed analysis.

As for international investment law, it is of interest because it actively develops the practice of direct application of general principles of law. This is noticeable in the decisions that investment tribunals have made, for example, in cases Phoenix Action, Saluka, Hrvatska Elektroprivreda and others. By analyzing the array of case-law in the field of investment disputes, it is possible to identify the principle of operation of the general principles of law, taking into account the needs of law enforcement, which in itself is valuable for the development of dispute resolution methods.

In addition, international investment law (both investment contracts and the judicial practice of tribunals), firstly, is largely aimed at protecting various obligations of private law reached between the host state and a foreign investor, and secondly, it has its own characteristics, which, when considered in parallel with international trade law (also part of international economic law), can be useful, in particular, decentralized origin, innovation combined with stability. For clarity, it is worth considering in more detail a number of international trade disputes and decisions of investment tribunals containing general principles of law.

Most of chosen principles are of interest due to their impact on disputes resolution and fundamentality of the general principles of law. However, for the global goal of this work which is treating of conflict of jurisdictions, only res judicata and lis pendens are crucial.

sub_elements.title: 2.1 Examples of the application of certain procedural general principles of law in international trade law
text: In the case of international trade law, the situation is even more complicated, since if we turn to WTO law, then we must take into account such an aspect as the autonomy of the set of norms. Despite the fierce debates of legal scholars, the principles can be perceived as norms, albeit with their own specifics, so the principles will also be included in the autonomous set of WTO law norms.

Attempts to apply directly some procedural general principles of law in the WTO practice have already been made, but such an approach was not duly considered since the WTO is very wary of innovations, which is quite fair, given the fragility of the world order. It is much more common to hear about some general principles of law indirectly rather than directly, as a rule, through the following mechanisms of interpretation of the DSU.

M. Panezi makes a noteworthy assumption. It is essential that there is no opinio juris, i.e. state practice, in such a source of WTO law as «customary practices» mentioned in article XVI.1 of the WTO Agreement. This fact brings «customary practices» closer to the general principles of law recognized by civilized nations and provides an additional «window of opportunity» for arbitrators regarding the application of general principles of law.

However, there is a more sophisticated way than the one proposed by M. Panezi. As follows from the text of the article 3.2 of Dispute Settlement Understanding of WTO (hereinafter – DSU) «to clarify the existing provisions of those agreements in accordance with customary rules of interpretation of public international law», and at the same time (ibid.): «Recommendations and rulings of the DSB cannot add to or diminish the rights and obligations provided in the covered agreements». As claimed D. Palmeter and P. Mavroidis, relying on the case United States - Standards for Reformulated and Conventional Gasoline, «the reference in Article 3.2 of the DSU has been held by the Appellate Body to refer to Articles 31 and 32 of the Vienna Convention, whose provisions become a source of law insofar as they reflect customary international law».

To be more specific, it is probably about subclause «C» of clause 1 of Article 32 that, along with the context, any relevant norms of international law applied in relations between the parties are taken into account when interpreting.

Regarding limitations of applicable law from article 3.2 of DSU a following solution was found by W. Weiss: «If the correct interpretation was that WTO courts could apply non-WTO international law in dispute resolution, this would not increase or decrease the rights and obligations of WTO members. And if international law, which goes beyond the well-known right of veto, is not applied independently in WTO disputes, then these provisions do not act as a conflict of laws rule».

McLachlan suggests that Article 31(3)(c) of the VCLT refers to ‘rules of law, and not to broader principles or considerations which may not be firmly established as rules. However, it is difficult to agree with this, since the principles, in essence, are rules. As A. D. Mitchell rightly points out, relying on case EC – Biotech, «WTO Tribunals have considered this issue, holding that all customary international law falls within the ‘rules of international law’ and that although it is less self-evident from the text of Article 31(3)(c), general principles of law must also be considered when interpreting a provision in accordance with the VCLT». Moreover, the Panel report text contains the following: «rules of international law’ must include all sources of international law, ‘that is to say, (i) international conventions (treaties), (ii) international custom (customary international law), and (iii) the recognized general principles of law». This only confirms the above thesis regarding applicable rules.

In addition, there is no prohibition on the use of sources of public international law, as the professor J. Pauwelyn says. The approach of J. Pauwelyn was continued by L. Bartels: «a principle of customary international law or a general principle of law could provide a defence to a WTO violation, even if the relevant international law principle was not reflected in any WTO provision».

Of course, it is not always beneficial for disputing countries to apply certain general principles of law in specific cases, or perhaps the participating countries simply want to maintain uniformity of law enforcement. So, for example, US claimed «General principles of international law’ were not listed in the covered agreements set forth in Appendix 1 of the DSU, and a WTO adjudicatory body had no authority to enforce those principle».

The following solutions exist as well - known examples of the use of general principles of law. In US – Shirts and Blouses, the Appellate Body found no rules on burden of proof in the DSU, so it used general principles of law under its inherent jurisdiction to supply them. Moreover, the Appellate Body added that, if a complainant ‘adduces evidence sufficient to raise a presumption that what is claimed is true, the burden then shifts to the other party, who will fail unless it adduces sufficient evidence to rebut the presumption’, describing this as «a generally-accepted canon of evidence in civil law, common law and, in fact, most jurisdictions».

A.D. Mitchell notes the following two criteria for the application of general principles of law in WTO practice. The first is a necessity recognized by a WTO Tribunal, the second is the principle must be used to resolve procedural matters and not as a source of substantive rights or obligations. In our case, this is a key point, since the author of this paper suggests using procedural general principles of law, realizing that too wide application is highly likely to exacerbate the crisis in law enforcement, further undermine confidence in WTO law, as it will expand judicial discretion.

One of the procedural general principles of law that can contribute to the fair and timely resolution of trade disputes are, for instance, res judicata and lis pendens. They were put in one sub-paragraph since the difference between them is quite slight and they apply to the same part of the case process, namely, its initiation.

As S. Nguyen notes, the essence of the res judicata principle is to prohibit the parties from re-trial in a case that has already been resolved, whereas the lis pendens principle regulates parallel proceedings, prescribing that during the consideration of one trial, it is not allowed to initiate another series of competing proceedings regarding the same dispute. Despite the fact that they are most often encountered in private law disputes, they can be as useful for public law as for private law.

Regarding the principle of lis pendens, the Judge Treves`s dissenting opinion in the Mox Plant case is of interest. He noted that «the legal status of this principle is open». Judge Travis probably meant that the practice of applying lis pendens is contradictory, especially in international law. Having a doctrinal foundation, the general principles of law look like a «fifth wheel», but this is not the case. Customs also do not appear in one day, this is the result of many years, and sometimes centuries-old practice. Nevertheless, the development of a fair and reasonable position through a comparative analysis of judicial practice and the exchange of international law enforcement experience can contribute to the formation and consolidation of an integral set of general principles of law.

So far, it remains to observe vivid cases in various international tribunals, courts and international investment disputes, the practice of the Court of Justice of the European Union, and just a description of some of the principles in WTO practice. For example, in the case of India – Autos the principle of res judicata was mentioned more than a hundred times in the report of the arbitration panel, and as a result, an approach was formulated consisting in comparing cases for the application of the principle. This is justified, since the essence of the case in which the proceedings are proceeding may not be identical to the case that arose in parallel and resembles the first one. In trade disputes, this is a separate problem, since some sub-fields of law are perceived as autonomous, with special regulation.

In paragraph 7.65 of the India – Measures Affecting the Automotive Sector Panel Report, based on the position of the WTO AB in the Guatemala – Cement case, the following was stated: «[the essence of the case] consists of two elements: the specific measures under consideration and the legal basis of the complaint (or claim). This seems to be the most appropriate minimum criterion for evaluating the possibility of fulfilling the conditions of res judicata…». The ambiguity and complexity of the application of general principles of law in this case is not a problem, on the contrary, the principle can contribute to the resolution of the conflict, but in these cases there was no application of res judicata, only careful reasoning took place. The WTO could be understood at that time, it was only the beginning of the activity of a young organization.

sub_elements.title: 2.1.2. Due process
text: In addition to res judicata and lis pendens, there are a number of equally significant procedural principles, one of which is due process. According to A.D. Mitchell, the history of this principle dates back to the Magna Carta of England, i.e. the XIII century, and was originally formed in the environment of criminal law, not civil. However, later this principle will become widespread due to its universality, reasonableness and, consequently, justice. By Amendments 5 and 14 of the US Constitution, the principle of due process was officially fixed, but was divided into substantive (which requires ‘legislation to be fair and reasonable in content and further a legitimate governmental objective) and procedural («which concerns the rights to notice, a hearing, counsel, appeal, and a fair and objective trial»). Of course, we will consider only the procedural side of this principle due to the reasons mentioned earlier.

Since due process is a general principle of law, it is not difficult to find similar provisions in Russian legislation. For example, Articles 46 and 123 of the Constitution of the Russian Federation state the following: «Everyone is guaranteed judicial protection of his rights and freedoms», «Judicial proceedings are carried out on the basis of adversarial and equal rights of the parties». Similar provisions can be found in the Arbitration Procedure Code of the Russian Federation, which specifies «fair public trial within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial court», as well as in the Civil Procedure Code and the Code of Administrative Procedure of the Russian Federation among the tasks of the proceedings.

The WTO law, by virtue of the presence of procedural procedures in it, also contains the provisions of the due process principle. Articles 11 and 12.7 of the DSU impose certain due process obligations on Panels, providing a concrete basis for the Appellate Body to review Panels’ conduct in this regard. According to A. D. Mitchell, who explained the essence of the principle within the WTO Law, due process in this field consists of two parts:
The matter before the panel (hearing rule);
Objective assessment (bias and no evidence rules);

In the case Chile – Price Band System the Appellate Body, firstly, agreed with Chile and found that Panel had acted inconsistently with Article 11 of the DSU by denying Chile the due process of a fair right of response and, secondly, explained the relationship between due process and Article 11 in a following way:

«Article 11 imposes duties on panels that extend beyond the requirement to assess evidence objectively and in good faith . . . This requirement is, of course, an indispensable aspect of a panel’s task. However, in making “an objective assessment of the matter before it”, a panel is also duty bound to ensure that due process is respected. Due process is an obligation inherent in the WTO dispute settlement system. A panel will fail in the duty to respect due process if it makes a finding on a matter that is not before it, because it will thereby fail to accord to a party a fair right of response. In this case, because the Panel did not give Chile a fair right of response on this issue, we find that the Panel failed to accord to Chile the due process rights to which it is entitled under the DSU» (para. 618). Similar formulations can be found in the US/Canada – Continued Suspension case.

International trade law does not end with a set of WTO rules, so it is worth paying attention to other jurisdictions and their experience in applying general principles of law. A potential object for comparison could be an autonomous set of norms of the European Union, in particular the European Court of Justice. However, it is unique in its essence and the general principles of law in this jurisdiction have their own specifics, expressed in the fact that, firstly, «the European Court of Justice refuses to apply general international law when it considers that its application would be incompatible with crucial elements for the structure of the EU legal order», Secondly, the general principles of law are an unwritten source of EU law, but on a par with status treaties. The comparison of WTO and EU law, even in a separate aspect, deserves rather a separate consideration. Nevertheless, despite the isolation of ECJ practice, even in it we can find an active application of the principles of res judicata, lis pendens and due process familiar to us.

Another set of legal norms considered part of international trade law is the law of the EAEU. Article 50 of the EAEU Court Statute presupposes the use of generally recognized principles and norms of international law in the practice of the court, but do these principles coincide with those specified in Article 38 of the Statute of the International Court of Justice? According to the Resolution of the Plenum of the Supreme Court of the Russian Federation No. 5 of October 10, 2003, «the generally recognized principles of international law should be understood as the fundamental peremptory norms of international law accepted and recognized by the international community of States as a whole, deviation from which is unacceptable» The interpretation of the word «generally recognized principles of law» for the conformity of this to «general principles of law» is contradictory, nevertheless, there is no prohibition to refer to the general principles of international law, so in some cases such references (see the case of Oil Marine Group) can be found, yet without specifying the principles. In this case, the situation is reversed in comparison with ECJ, since the source is written, but is not used as widely as in the European Union.

Thus, such general principles of law as res judicata, lis pendens, due process are among the key links of the procedural order of the WTO law, which confirms the thesis of the importance of this source of law in the framework of dispute resolution in the WTO system, including through the above-mentioned judicial practice. Despite the active resistance of some states in individual cases regarding the application of general principles of law as a source, the WTO case-law allows their application with a significant restriction, which is expressed in the use of only procedural general principles of law, so as not to violate the substantive rights of the disputing parties.

As for other jurisdictions dealing with disputes in the field of international trade, for instance, EU law and the practice of the European Court of Justice could be of interest, but only in a narrow sense, since the set of EU norms has its own specifics of application, namely, it applies the «general principles of community law», i.e. the source used has a regional character. Consequently, the main source of empirical material for subsequent comparison with international investment law remains the law and practice of the World Trade Organization.

sub_elements.title: 2.2 Examples of the application of certain procedural general principles of law in international investment law
2.2.1 Burden of proof
text: Regarding the principle of the burden of proof, there are a number of arguments of some investment tribunals, which indicate that they consider this concept as a general principle of international law. Its essence lies in the fact that the plaintiff must prove the fact upon which it relies (onus probandi incumbit actori). Moreover, some decisions simply noted that this principle exists in accordance with international law or similar formulations were applied.

In this context, it is worth mentioning P. Dumberry's remark that the Tribunals are not inclined to make references to various rules of substantive law of States in support of the concept of a general principle of law, which can be traced in the logic of the Salini case. This case is also notable for the fact that burden of proof is recognized by the Tribunal as a well-established principle of law, as evidenced by the practice of The Permanent Court of International Justice, the International Court of Justice, ICSID. Moreover, some investment tribunals arbitrators have even considered that the principle is a fundamental concept of the international legal community.

However, there is a plethora of approaches to the principle with regard to shifting of the burden from one party to another. For instance, in the cases US – Shirts and Blouses (previously mentioned in the Chapter 2) and AAPL v. Sri Lanka and Marvin Feldman v. Mexico it was outlined by tribunals and by Appellate body that the burden of proof can be shifted when a prima facie case has been established. At the same time in the case Rompetrol vs Romania it was explicitly and strictly noted that the burden of proof shall not be shifted to another party since it is unhelpful and confusing for the proceedings.

Therefore, the uncertain enforcement of such procedural general principle of law as burden of proof shall be considered, in author`s opinion, as a caveat of the possible judicial discretion through the variety of approaches. It will be mentioned later as a risk since uniformity in perception is crucial for enforcement and not in all cases it may be reached.

sub_elements.title: 2.2.2 Estoppel principle and its correlation to good faith principle
text: Referring to such a general principle of law as Estoppel or the prohibition of contradictory behavior, it can be noted that this principle is common to Muslim, Anglo-Saxon and continental legal systems. This is a confirmation of the degree of reasonableness that is both obvious and at the same time fair for each participant in the dispute. References to the principle of consistent behavior have been traced since the ancient Roman period of the development of law.

In the case of Amco Asia Corporation et al v Indonesia, the tribunal also recognized the fact that «the concept [of the Estoppel principle] is based on Anglo-Saxon law» but added that «it is based on the fundamental requirement of good faith, which is found in all legal systems, both national and international». However, despite the widespread and well-known popularity of this principle, it will be impossible to use it in the current study due to the following. As case-law and doctrine indicate, the application of the Estoppel principle is very limited in international trade law, so we will focus only on mentioning.

sub_elements.title: 2.2.3 Res judicata and lis pendens. Forum non conveniens
text: As for the principle of res judicata, then, as S. Nguyen notes, the essence is to prohibit the parties from re-trial in a case that has already been decided, whereas the principle of lis pendens regulates parallel proceedings, prescribing that during the consideration of one trial, it is not allowed to initiate another series of competing proceedings regarding the same dispute. Older tribunals regarded res judicata as an «established rule of law», a «principle of international law», «an essential and well-established norm of international law», an «established» principle in international proceedings. There are more questions and risks regarding lis pendens, as doubts are expressed about the involvement of this principle as a whole in general international law. A logical addition and expression of the lis pendens principle is the practice of the «fork in the road» provision in some bilateral investment agreements, when the parties must choose only one forum to resolve the dispute, such as in the case of Genin vs Estonia.

We should also not forget about the doctrine of forum non conveniens (a practical expression of the principle of lis pendens), which is a flexible technique to handle parallel proceedings. If the claim abroad has been filed for tactical reasons and is at an early stage, the court might consider itself to be the appropriate forum, but there are quite a few limitations of this practice. Thus, the principle of lis pendens, the provision in the «fork in the road» contracts and the doctrine of forum non conveniens should be separated.

Also, there is ambiguity when using the lis pendens principle in the context of distinction between contract and treaty claims. For example, in the case of GAMI Investments Inc. v Mexico raised this issue in the context of the correlation of national and international law. The main problem was the uncertainty about the appropriate remedies. If the contract was violated, then national law remedies are applied, and if treaty – international law ones. However, in practice this is not always obvious. As a consequence, the uncertainty in this issue generates distrust during proceedings, so the principle of lis pendens is subject to careful study for further application in practice.

It is noteworthy that for post-soviet space, the general principles of law are also not a hollow sound. For example, in the ICSID Award of 2012 Bosh v. Ukraine, there are provisions that address the violation of the res judicata principle, which is, firstly, a recognized general principle of law (according to the Tribunal, paragraph 277 of Award), and secondly, part of the Ukrainian Code of Commercial Procedure (paragraph 278 of Award).
The doctrine and practice of international investment tribunals reveal the five components of the principle of res judicata:
same legal order;
same parties;
same object (petitum);
same ground of case (causa petendi); and
finality and binding effect of the prior award.

Items 2-4 make up the well-known «the triple identity test». The essence of the «same legal order» is that res judicata may apply in the context of international arbitration only if both the preceding and subsequent proceedings share the same legal order. «Same parties» means identity of the parties between the previous and subsequent disputes before applying res judicata.

This point is a little bit complicated due to affiliation estimation and different approaches to it. And, finally, «same ground of case» presupposes both identical object and ground of a claim. Thus, despite the fact that the general principles of law are most often found in private law disputes, they can be as useful for public law as for private law, but with a number of reservations. This thesis will be revealed in the next chapter.

Also, an equally significant phenomenon that should be borne in mind with respect to the procedural general principles of law is a decrease in the share of attention to the origin of the general principle of law, to be more precise, to which legal order it belongs whether international or national. The author of the paper believes that this is a large waste of highly-qualified labor for the outdated case-law, since at the moment there is an extensive database of cases, on the basis of which it is possible to judge how widely we apply this or that general principle and, therefore, use it directly.

table_of_content.title: Chapter 3 Risks and prospects of applying general principles of law by analogy
text: As noted in previous chapters, there are many general principles of law that fill in the gaps in different sectors of international disputes. Responding to a fair objection about the flexibility and vagueness of the general principles of law, the author of this paper presents a thesis on the application of only procedural general principles of law with a thorough preliminary study of the experience and legal grounds for their application and taking into account the opinions of the parties that this source will meet in practice in the future. Now it is necessary to highlight in more detail the prospects for the application of procedural general principles of law and to illustrate the meaning of drawing analogies of international trade and investment sub-fields of law.

As it was mentioned earlier, the purpose of the general principles of law is to cover legal gaps (lacunae), which is especially in demand when resolving the issue of conflict of jurisdictions. International trade law is no exception in this case. As one of the striking procedural conflicts in international trade law, which could be resolved with the help of procedural general principles of law, is the conflict of WTO and RTA jurisdictions.

The conflict is generated by the presence of simultaneously coexisting agreements of the parties, one of them is the WTO Agreement, the other is the RTA agreement. Most RTAs have their own dispute resolution mechanisms, one way or another similar to the WTO mechanism, but the parties are given a choice between these jurisdictions, as for example, in the Agreement on the Creation of the North American Free Trade Area – NAFTA (expired due to the establishment of CUSMA in 2018-2019).

As V.A. Bublik and A.V. Gubareva point out, RTA mechanisms are considered the most effective for resolving regional and interregional disputes, but they are not often addressed, since agreements regulate the rules for granting concessions related to market access, and disagreements arise extremely rarely about these rules and their implementation. In addition, in 2006 there was an opinion that the RTAs are not able to ensure the rapid appointment of independent arbitrators, the involvement of third-party experts, the execution of decisions rendered, the possibility of appeal, however, from that date the RTA practice has developed and there are several disputes that were recently successfully resolved within RTA mechanisms, for instance, in case Ukraine – Export Prohibition on Wood Products, Southern African Customs Union – Safeguard Measure Imposed on Frozen Bone-In Chicken Cuts from the European Union and some others.

table_of_content.title: Conclusion
text: Summing up the results of the study, the following conclusions can be drawn. Firstly, the general principles of law are an essential part of international economic law, although they are not always applied directly. Recently, this source has been subjected to a thorough analysis by the International Law Commission (as many as 3 voluminous reports have been prepared over the past four years), which made it possible to find out what metamorphoses the general principles of law underwent, what elements they consist of and where they came from. So, among the key provisions are the following:
1) the general principles of law are initially fundamental for all legal systems and demonstrate the essential and reasonable unity of the law;
2) they may be considered as a threat due to extended autonomy and discretion of judges;
3) they have a system-forming, fundamental character for both public and private international law;
4) they have a number of functions, where one of the most important is the «prohibition of non-liquid» or filling in legal gaps;
5) as the Special Rapporteur noted, the construct «civilized nations» is an anachronism, since at the moment this discriminatory division is not taken into account;
6) there is a division of the general principles of law recognized by civilized nations into substantive (material) and procedural.

The use of the procedural general principles in law enforcement in international economic law is considered by the author of the paper as a possible key to the harmonious and logical development of this sub-field of law. However, it may be called an ideal solution only barely due to the several mentioned reasons.

Secondly, international trade law, as a sub-field with a dominant share of a public nature, is less flexible and adaptive, therefore, it is not necessary to speak about the large-scale application of procedural general principles of law in this area, as evidenced by a number of the cases mentioned above. Nevertheless, there are also unique cases, for example, an active study of the applicability of the general principle of law by the WTO body in the cases of India – Measures Affecting the Automotive Sector, United States – Wool Shirts and Blouses, Guatemala – Cement and some other cases.

Thirdly, not all judicial bodies in international trade law (in particular, the integration associations of the EU and the EAEU) can be perceived as full-fledged forums for assessing and applying general principles of law due to their specifics, despite the availability of opportunities to apply this source directly or indirectly. As for the European Union, this confederation's perception of the general principles of law is conditioned by internal law enforcement, which can distort the empirical material for further analysis.

Fourthly, in the international investment law, the situation is different than with international trade law. The general principles of law are actively used in investment tribunals. Such cases as Phoenix Action (the principle of good faith), Saluka (unjustified enrichment) Hrvatska Elektroprivreda, Soufraki (competence-competence), Petrobart (prohibition to invoke domestic law for the sake of evading international obligations), etc. are used as quite vivid and profound examples.

The application of general principles of law in this sub-field allows us to find a significant layer for further research and implementation. In particular, regarding res judicata, there is a whole test that allows us to judge the applicability of this general principle of law. At the same time regarding lis pendens, there are also disputes concerning its integrity primarily due to its diverse nature, which includes the practice of fork in the road, the doctrine of forum non conveniens (which is in nutshell a practical expression of lis pendens), therefore, as has been repeatedly noted in the doctrine, the principle is used as a tool in tribunals, but it requires further elaboration.

Fifthly, the author of the work considers the position found in the Mobil Award case about the absence of the need to look for the roots of the general principle of law (domestic or international) progressive and key for the further development of this source, since, in essence, the reasonableness and prevalence of a particular general principle of law in the world affects the fairness and legitimacy of the decision, and not its origin.

Summarizing the above, it can be concluded that the general principles of law are actively used as a fair instrument for resolving international disputes, despite the fact that not everywhere the general principles of law have won approval. At least to call them «wall flowers on the walls of international law» is extremely unwise. Consequently, the application of some of the above–mentioned procedural general principles of law in international trade law based on international investment disputes could contribute to the resolution of many disputes, for example, at one time in the case of Mexico - soft drinks.

The conflict of jurisdictions in this case and some others previously mentioned could be resolved relatively simply with the help of such procedural general principles of law as lis pendens or res judicata. At the same time, each such principle should be analyzed and weighed, since it may contain contradictions, like lis pendens, or expand the scope of judicial review too much and provoke distrust of the parties and only aggravate the conflict.

However, it is also worth taking into account the specifics of the WTO autonomous complex of legal norms and the political background of the Marrakesh Agreements. The agreements reached may turn out to be too fragile for such experiments, therefore, the introduction of procedural general principles of law is considered first of all from a theoretical point of view, as a likely alternative since the WTO dispute resolution system was significantly slowed down due to the crisis and political events.
